author: @stevetobias4890
text:
Excellent Rob, add a core to the coil so that the magnets interact with it every pass. For those unaware, when a magnet passes an iron core, that core becomes a temporary magnet. Having the magnets in an alternating N S N S orientation the core temporarily becomes an alternating N S N S magnet which passes the magnetic resonance to the core which gives you the output current.
I like the way you used neodymium magnets and the spacing is good for a wind generator because of the speed the wheel rotates having no load. Very cool potential here, especially if you tried the microwave transformers with the coil close to the magnets.
Cheers Rob.

replies.author: @NorthernKitty
replies.text: @colleenforrest7936 My 20" mower actually uses batteries that cost less than $100.  It uses two 12V x 22Ah lead acid batteries connected in series for 24V.  I buy them on Amazon, "Mighty Max Battery ML22-12" for about $85.   They generally last me 5-6 years before I have to replace them, and manage to mow about 1/4 acre (the size of my yard) before they need recharging.  I actually bought an extra set to use for a portable 400Wh inverter system I put together on a hand cart, which also means I have spares in case the batteries in my mower die.  But I'm guessing you may have been meaning Lithium batteries?

replies.author: @NorthernKitty
replies.text: Most of them are very simply built, with just heating element(s) and a switch.  However, the switches can be pretty ingenious.  I watched a tear down of one on YouTube where the cooker could switch automatically from "cook" to "keep warm".  Switching it to "keep warm" would simply turn on a small heating element.  But when you switched it to "cook", a larger element was turned on and a metal dongle would be pressed to collapse a spring until a magnet caught the dongle and held it in place.  When the magnet became too hot - presumably reaching a temperature at which the rice would be thoroughly cooked - it lost its magnetism and could no longer hold the dongle.  The spring would then force the dongle out, which turned off the "cook" switch, thus leaving it in "keep warm" mode.  A simple yet brilliant solution.

author: @Xx-xd3zo
text:
You know, the way that looks and the way it spins?  It's similar to what you see in jet turbines.  I'm never certain as to why it's not used in wind turbines.  Seems to capture so much air/wind/energy with that kind of blade coverage.
I assume they've done tons of tests, but can't help but wonder.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: I agree mate -it does make you wonder for sure

replies.author: @MAGnetICus_Attractus
replies.text: Are you talking about the primary inlet blades?  Cause that bike does not look like compressor blades in a jet engine

replies.author: @ThisRandomUsername
replies.text: I'd guess is that typical wind turbines are designed based off a cost vs power output tradeoff. A more complex turbine will have more material than a similarly sized 3-blade one and not produce that much more power. In other words you could sweep a much larger uninterrupted air stream with a 3-blade design of the same weight, and therefore convert more power.

replies.author: @Xx-xd3zo
replies.text: @ThisRandomUsername - I did see a good video where they had about 5 turbines lined up.  They all had the same motor/generators and then different fan or blade configurations.  Some had three, some had five, and then I think one had larger/longer blades.  They had the same fans pointed at them - decently controlled.  And the longer 3 blade, ie. diameter won (like you say.)  I can't remember if the....I think the 3 blade was better than the 5 of same size.  Not sure.  Wasn't a bad little experiment/video.

replies.author: @ThisRandomUsername
replies.text: @Xx-xd3zo Very interesting. I'll look that up later, and post if I find something.

author: @AtlasReburdened
text: It's a shame that you haven't found any of the high grade ones yet. There are some out there that contain powerful low RPM generators and provide digitally controllable resistance via a bank of MOSFETS shunting the power generated through some seriously beefy resistors, varied via PWM. When I was repairing gym equipment for a living we would occasionally do a home call for some ritzy customer who had more money than brains and would demand that every internal component be replaced including the generator(despite my insistence that this was unnecessary), and the company I worked for had a policy that everything that was pulled out got sold to the scrap yard. So I just paid them 50% over scrap rate for the generators and wound up with two that can pump out as much power as a human can possibly pump in. You could probably land a similar find if you find a gym equipment repair company and either work out a deal to pay them over scrap rate for whatever you want to pull off their discarded machines, or by inquiring about what scrap yard they use and working out a similar deal with them. I've had a lot of luck in scrapyards. They're usually pretty amicable to the idea of someone taking things to bits and sorting them without having to be paid, and then giving them over scrap rate for the bits they want.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: very nice tips mate - I must find a scrap yard

replies.author: @AtlasReburdened
replies.text: @Alternative Space Misc.  Well, when I was in my prime(this was as a _particularly_ fit Marine) I could generate ~350W continuous for about 20 minutes, so I'd say the average fit person could manage 150-175W over that span. This would equate to 50-55Wh per session, per person, so 5 of those sessions would equate to 250-275Wh per day, per person.

replies.author: @AtlasReburdened
replies.text: @alternativespacemisc.8429 Yeah... There _is_ a very good reason that we generate real power via the combustion of hydrocarbon fuels. Humans just don't have the throughput or power density of fuels.

replies.author: @AtlasReburdened
replies.text: @alternativespacemisc.8429 Well, I'd say it's certainly possible to run decent ventilation off of the output and still have enough leftover to offer 'free' USB charging to everyone.
